summary: This page provides diagnostic guidance for wire welding related to workpiece heat control and weld penetration. It includes a diagram showing three penetration states (inadequate, proper, excess/burn-through) with corresponding methods to increase or decrease workpiece heat and penetration. A second section, "Example Wire Weld Diagrams," shows six weld bead cross-sections and profiles illustrating good welds versus common defects (voltage/wire feed too low or high, travel speed too fast or slow, and CTWD too long or wrong polarity), each with correction instructions.
text:
Wire Weld Diagnosis–Workpiece Heat Control / Weld Penetration

INADEQUATE PENETRATION | PROPER PENETRATION | EXCESS PENETRATION OR BURN-THROUGH

Not hot enough -------- Ideal heat -------- Too hot

How to increase workpiece heat and increase penetration: (to weld THICKER workpieces properly)
a. Increase weld current
b. Decrease travel speed
c. Use faster wire feed
d. Use shorter CTWD

How to reduce workpiece heat and limit penetration: (to weld THINNER workpieces properly)
e. Decrease weld current
f. Increase travel speed
g. Use slower wire feed
h. Use longer CTWD

Example Wire Weld Diagrams

Good Weld
- (no defect; well-formed weld bead and penetration)

Voltage Too Low or Wire Feed Too Slow
TO CORRECT: Increase Output Voltage or Increase Wire Feed Speed

Voltage Too High or Wire Feed Too Fast
TO CORRECT: Decrease Output Voltage or Decrease Wire Feed Speed

Travel Speed Too Fast
TO CORRECT: travel slower

Travel Speed Too Slow
TO CORRECT: travel faster

CTWD Too Long or Wrong Polarity
TO CORRECT: Check Polarity and maintain less than 1/2" CTWD

Item 57812 — For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353. — Page 35

figures.caption: Wire Weld Diagnosis–Workpiece Heat Control / Weld Penetration
figures.description: Three cross-section diagrams of weld beads on a workpiece showing: (1) Inadequate Penetration - a shallow weld bead sitting on top with a gap/crack visible below in the base metal, labeled 'Not hot enough'; (2) Proper Penetration - a weld bead with a deep, well-fused triangular penetration profile into the base metal, labeled 'Ideal heat'; (3) Excess Penetration or Burn-Through - a weld with a large rounded penetration profile extending far into/through the base metal, labeled 'Too hot'. A double-headed arrow spans below all three from 'Not hot enough' to 'Too hot' with 'Ideal heat' in the middle. Below are two columns of corrective actions: left column (a-d) for increasing heat/penetration to weld thicker workpieces, right column (e-h) for reducing heat/penetration to weld thinner workpieces.

figures.caption: Example Wire Weld Diagrams
figures.description: Six side-by-side illustrations, each showing a top-down/cross-section view of a weld bead above a corresponding side-profile cutaway showing weld ripple pattern and penetration depth. From left to right: (1) 'Good Weld' - smooth uniform bead with consistent penetration and no spatter; (2) 'Voltage Too Low or Wire Feed Too Slow' - small rounded bead sitting high with shallow, uneven penetration; correction: increase output voltage or increase wire feed speed; (3) 'Voltage Too High or Wire Feed Too Fast' - wide flat bead with excessive spatter (dots) around it and deep uneven penetration; correction: decrease output voltage or decrease wire feed speed; (4) 'Travel Speed Too Fast' - narrow bead with tight ripple pattern and shallow penetration; correction diagram shows torch moving with 'travel slower' arrow; (5) 'Travel Speed Too Slow' - wide bead with excessive buildup and wide penetration; correction diagram shows torch with 'travel faster' arrow; (6) 'CTWD Too Long or Wrong Polarity' - irregular bead with heavy spatter (dots) and inconsistent penetration; correction diagram shows torch held at an angle with arrow indicating 'Check Polarity and maintain less than 1/2" CTWD'.

key_facts: To weld thicker workpieces properly (increase heat/penetration): increase weld current, decrease travel speed, use faster wire feed, or use shorter CTWD.
To weld thinner workpieces properly (reduce heat/penetration): decrease weld current, increase travel speed, use slower wire feed, or use longer CTWD.
Inadequate penetration indicates the weld is 'not hot enough'; proper penetration is the 'ideal heat'; excess penetration/burn-through indicates the weld is 'too hot'.
Voltage too low or wire feed too slow: correct by increasing output voltage or increasing wire feed speed.
Voltage too high or wire feed too fast: correct by decreasing output voltage or decreasing wire feed speed.
Travel speed too fast: correct by traveling slower.
Travel speed too slow: correct by traveling faster.
CTWD too long or wrong polarity: correct by checking polarity and maintaining less than 1/2" CTWD.
